narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 55% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including your Year 1 school decile of 4—below the typical 5.7 average—and especially your Year 3 decile of 1, which is much lower than the usual 5.5. Your LSAT score of 34 also falls short of the average 36.7, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 is under the standard 3.2. Coming from an upper-middle income family is fairly typical, and as a full-time White female applicant, your demographics align closely with most students in our dataset. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 performance, which pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure more than any other factor. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, followed by your LSAT score, each contributing to raise the risk slightly. Your family income level, undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller influences, all nudging the odds a bit toward failure but not as decisively. Overall, your profile shows academic metrics below averages that the model weighs heavily for bar success. To improve your chances next time, focus on strengthening your academic foundation—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, as it's a changeable factor, and build a stronger recent performance record through additional coursework or prep programs. Demonstrating upward trends in grades could help offset those early deciles in future evaluations. With targeted effort on these areas, you could shift closer to the profiles of students who typically pass the bar. Keep pushing forward; many overcome similar hurdles with persistence.
